name: Al-Shifa bint Abdullah
title: Al-Shifa bint Abdullah (RA): The Healer and the Teacher
meta_description: The story of Al-Shifa bint Abdullah (RA), one of the earliest Muslims: a healer, one of the few women in Makkah who could read and write, and a trusted advisor to the Prophet Muhammad and to Umar.
keywords: Al-Shifa bint Abdullah, story of Al-Shifa, women of the sahaba, literacy in Islam, healer, teacher of Hafsa, sahaba stories
lede: In a city where almost no one could read, a woman sat with a pen and taught others to hold one. She knew medicine, she knew letters, and she knew the careful art of writing well. People stopped calling her by her given name and called her the healer.

This is the story of Al-Shifa bint Abdullah (may Allah be pleased with her): one of the first to believe, a teacher of reading and writing, and a woman the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ and later Umar both turned to for counsel.

sections.h2: The woman they called the healer
paras: Her given name was Leila. She was born into Quraysh, from the tribe of Banu Adi, the same clan as Umar ibn al-Khattab (RA), to whom she was related. She married Abu Hathma ibn Hudhayfah, and together they had two sons, Sulayman and Masruq.

She is one of those companions whose name many of us have never heard, even though she stood among the first. Her biography survives in only a handful of accounts. Yet within those few pages is a life worth slowing down for, the kind of quiet life that holds a community together without ever asking to be noticed.

People did not remember her by the name Leila. They called her al-Shifa, which means the healer, because she had learned medicine and used it to treat the sick. The nickname stayed with her for the rest of her life, until it became the only name most people knew. A name is usually given to you; hers was earned by what she did for others.

She is sometimes spoken of as if she came later, as if she belonged to the generation after the companions, because the role she is most famous for came after the Prophet ﷺ had passed away. But her place in Islam runs much deeper than that. Her reputation came before Islam, and she was among the very first people to accept the message when it arrived.

sections.h2: One of the few who could read
paras: Makkah at that time was a city of speech, not of writing. Most people could not read at all. By the count of the biographers, only around twenty to thirty people in the whole city knew how to read and write, and al-Shifa was one of them.

She did more than read. She was a calligrapher, someone who could shape letters with care and beauty, and she taught that skill to others. So she carried three uncommon things at once: she could heal the body, she could read and write, and she had the patience to pass both on. Any one of these would have set her apart. She held all three.

She was known for her wisdom too, her hikma, the kind of judgement people come back to. We do not know the exact moment she accepted Islam, but we know it was within the first three years of the Prophet's call, and she was among the first women to make the Hijrah to Madinah. Her faith was not a late arrival. It was there at the very beginning, when believing was hard and the believers were few.

sections.h2: A house between the mosque and the market
paras: In Madinah she was given a home in a place that suited her exactly: between the mosque and the marketplace. It became a well-known house in the city, a quiet meeting point with worship on one side and daily trade on the other. It was the right address for a woman who understood both prayer and the affairs of ordinary life.

The Prophet ﷺ trusted her judgement and took her counsel in many matters, especially questions of money and the marketplace, where she had a particular understanding. That is worth pausing on. He sought her advice not for small things, but for the workings of the community. He would visit her family from time to time, and she lived for those visits.

So she prepared. She kept a special wrap and blanket set aside, amenities meant only for him, ready for the times he would come, hoping quietly that he would come more often. When he did, he would put on what she had prepared and take his rest in their home. To host him, to learn from him, to be asked by him: this was the honour of her life, and she arranged her whole household around it.

Al-Shifa was a woman of means. She had her own standing, a livelihood she had built in Makkah, and an inheritance from her marriage. By the measure most people use, she had plenty to leave behind. When she died, none of that was counted as her treasure.

Her most precious belongings, the things she left for her sons Sulayman and Masruq to inherit, were the clothes and the blanket the Prophet ﷺ had used when he visited her home. Not the wealth she had earned. Not what she had inherited. The cloth he had rested in.

It is a small detail, but it tells you everything about what she valued. The most ordinary objects in her house had become the most precious, simply because of who had touched them. While she was alive, her heart had already done the sorting, and quietly decided that nearness to the Prophet ﷺ outweighed everything she owned.

sections.h2: Teaching Hafsa to read and to heal
paras: One of her closest students was Hafsa (RA), the wife of the Prophet ﷺ and a relative of al-Shifa through the family of Umar. Al-Shifa taught Hafsa to read, to write, and to do calligraphy, passing her own rare skills into the next pair of hands.

There is an authentic narration in Abu Dawud where the Prophet ﷺ came in upon al-Shifa and Hafsa as they sat together. He said to al-Shifa: will you not teach Hafsa how to treat the sore of an ant bite, the same way you taught her to write? She was a healer who knew several kinds of treatment, and he approved of her continuing so long as it stayed grounded in what came from the Quran and the Sunnah, and not in the practices of the days before Islam.

This carries a weight that is easy to miss. The Quran was later gathered and kept in the house of Hafsa, and some scholars connect that to her literacy, since so few could read at all. The woman who taught Hafsa her letters had a quiet hand in that, and she could never have known it at the time. A teacher rarely sees how far a single lesson will travel.

sections.h2: Trusted by Umar, and the morning prayer
paras: After the Prophet ﷺ passed away, al-Shifa kept her home, and her counsel was still sought. When Umar (RA) became caliph, he would visit her house on his way to the marketplace, just as he had seen the Prophet ﷺ do. He consulted her often on the fiqh of trade and the affairs of the market, and it is even reported that he gave her a role overseeing it.

One morning Umar asked her why her son Sulayman, a man known for his recitation and his long nights of prayer, had not been present for the Fajr prayer in congregation. She answered that Sulayman had prayed through the night and had fallen asleep just before dawn.

Umar's reply has been remembered ever since. To pray Fajr in congregation, he said, is more beloved to me than praying the whole night alone. He was recalling the Prophet's words: whoever prays Isha in congregation, it is as if he prayed half the night, and whoever prays Fajr in congregation, it is as if he prayed the whole night.

It is striking that this measured, much-loved teaching reaches us through her. She was the one in the room, the one who answered the question about her son, and so her account preserved the moment. Her home was not only a place of rest for the Prophet ﷺ; it was a place where things worth remembering were said and kept.

Her sons both served. Masruq was appointed as a governor and known for his knowledge. Through Sulayman her line continued, and he named his own two sons Abu Bakr and Uthman, who grew up to narrate hadith. That is part of why her words survived at all: a learned grandmother, and grandchildren careful enough to carry what she said.

Among the things she narrated is the famous account of how Umar came to be called Amir al-Mu'minin, the commander of the believers. Two men sent from Iraq came to Madinah and addressed him by that title at the door of the mosque. Umar said they had been guided to the right name: he was the commander, and the believers were the believers. From that day the name stayed. It reached us through a conversation between a grandmother and her grandson.

She lived long into the time of Umar and then passed away, leaving behind hadith recorded by Bukhari, Abu Dawud, and al-Nasa'i. When she died, it was Umar himself who led the funeral prayer over her, this early believer, healer, and teacher. May Allah be pleased with her.

lessons_intro: Her story is not built on a single famous moment. It is built on useful skill, given away patiently, in service of others.

lessons.bold: Knowledge is meant to be shared.
lessons.text: She did not guard her literacy or her medicine. She taught them, and what she taught outlived her by generations.
She taught one woman to read. Through that, she touched the keeping of the Quran itself. We rarely see where a single lesson lands.

lessons.bold: Treasure what is sacred, not what is costly.
lessons.text: Of all she owned, she left her children a worn blanket, because it had touched the Prophet ﷺ. Her heart valued the right things.
Two of the greatest men consulted her on serious matters. She did not seek attention; her usefulness and wisdom spoke for themselves.

faq.a: One of the earliest Muslims, from the tribe of Banu Adi and a relative of Umar ibn al-Khattab (RA). She was a healer, one of the few literate women in Makkah, and a teacher who later advised the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ and Umar on the affairs of the marketplace.
Her given name was Leila. Al-Shifa, meaning the healer, was a nickname she earned because she knew medicine and treated the sick. It stayed with her until it became the name people knew her by.
Al-Shifa taught Hafsa (RA), the wife of the Prophet ﷺ, how to read, write, and do calligraphy, and the Prophet ﷺ also asked her to teach Hafsa a way of treating the sore of an ant bite.

faq.q: What can we learn from the life of Al-Shifa?
faq.a: That knowledge is meant to be shared, that a small act of teaching can carry further than we ever see, and that quiet, useful service is honoured even when it never seeks attention.

closing_heading: Why this story stays with us
closing: Most of us will not be remembered for one dramatic act. We will be remembered, if at all, for what we patiently gave: a skill passed on, a person taught, a small service done again and again. Al-Shifa's whole life is a quiet argument that this is enough, and more than enough. She healed, she taught, she kept a corner of her home ready for a guest she loved, and through those ordinary acts she shaped people and preserved words that reached us across fourteen centuries.

There is something she carried that we can carry today: the willingness to teach what we know, and to value the sacred over the expensive. If there is something you understand that someone near you needs, give it, without keeping count and without waiting to be thanked. You may never see where it travels. She never did either, and look how far it went.
source_note: The narrative follows the account of Al-Shifa bint Abdullah (RA) in Dr. Omar Suleiman's series The Firsts (Yaqeen Institute). Her hadith are recorded by Bukhari, Abu Dawud, and al-Nasa'i. Where the historical record carries more than one narration, we have followed the most widely reported.

slides:
In a city where almost no one could read, a woman sat with a pen and taught others to hold one.

Her name was Leila. People called her al-Shifa: the healer.

Makkah was a city of speech, not writing.

By the count of the biographers, only around twenty to thirty people in the whole city could read and write.

She was one of them.

She knew three uncommon things at once.

Medicine. Letters. And calligraphy, which she taught to others.

She was among the very first to accept Islam.

In Madinah her home sat between the mosque and the marketplace.

The Prophet ﷺ trusted her counsel, and would rest in her house when he visited.

She kept a wrap and a blanket set aside, ready, only for him.

When she died, that worn cloth was her treasure, not her wealth. She left it to her sons.

She taught Hafsa (RA) to read and to write.

The Quran was later gathered and kept in Hafsa's house.

A teacher rarely sees how far one lesson will travel.

Years later, Umar (RA) still came to her home to seek her counsel on the marketplace, as he had seen the Prophet ﷺ do.

When she died, Umar led her funeral prayer.

**Knowledge is meant to be shared.**

She taught what she knew, and it outlived her by generations.

May Allah be pleased with her.
social.caption:
She was one of the earliest Muslims, but you may never have heard her name. Al-Shifa bint Abdullah (may Allah be pleased with her) was a healer and one of the very few women in Makkah who could read and write.

She taught Hafsa (RA), the wife of the Prophet ﷺ, to read, to write, and to do calligraphy, and the Quran was later kept in Hafsa's house. The Prophet ﷺ trusted her counsel, and Umar (RA) later consulted her on the affairs of the marketplace. When she died, he led her funeral prayer himself.

What would you want to pass on to someone near you? 🤍

Save this for later, and share it with someone who would love it.

email.subject: The woman who taught Makkah to read
email.alt_subject: One of the few who could read, in a city of speech
email.preview: She knew medicine and letters in a city where almost no one could write. Her name was al-Shifa.
email.body:
Assalamu alaikum,

In the Makkah of that time, almost no one could read. By the count of the biographers, only around twenty to thirty people in the whole city knew how to write. One of them was a woman.

Her given name was Leila, but people called her al-Shifa (may Allah be pleased with her), which means the healer, because she knew medicine and treated the sick. She was also a calligrapher, and she taught others to read and to write. She carried three uncommon things at once: she could heal, she could read and write, and she had the patience to pass both on. She was among the very first to accept Islam, and among the first women to make the Hijrah to Madinah.

In Madinah her home sat between the mosque and the marketplace. The Prophet ﷺ trusted her judgement, sought her counsel on the affairs of the market, and would rest in her house when he visited. She kept a wrap and a blanket set aside, ready, only for him. When she died, that worn cloth was the treasure she left her sons, not her wealth. Her heart had already chosen what mattered.

She taught Hafsa (RA), the wife of the Prophet ﷺ, to read and to write. The Quran was later gathered and kept in Hafsa's house. The woman who taught Hafsa her letters had a quiet hand in that, though she could not have known it. A teacher rarely sees how far one lesson will travel.

Years later, when Umar (RA) became caliph, he still came to her home to ask her counsel on the affairs of the marketplace, just as he had seen the Prophet ﷺ do. And when she died, it was Umar himself who led her funeral prayer.

There is a lesson in her that is easy to miss: knowledge is meant to be shared. She did not guard what she knew. She gave it away, patiently, and it outlived her by generations. If you understand something that someone near you needs, give it.

May Allah be pleased with her, and let us pass on what good we have been given.

If you would like the full story, we have written it here:

paras: In a city where almost no one could read, there was a woman who sat with a pen and taught others to hold one. Her given name was Leila, but the people of Makkah called her al-Shifa (may Allah be pleased with her), which means the healer, because she knew medicine and treated the sick.

She carried three uncommon things at once. She knew medicine, she could read and write when only a handful of people in the whole city could, and she was a calligrapher who taught the careful shaping of letters. She was among the very first to accept Islam, and among the first women to make the journey to Madinah.

In Madinah she was given a home between the mosque and the marketplace. The Prophet ﷺ trusted her counsel, especially in matters of trade, and from time to time he would visit her family. She lived for those visits, and kept a wrap and a blanket set aside, ready, only for him, hoping he would come more often.

When she died, none of her wealth was counted as her treasure. Her most precious belongings, the things she left her sons, were the cloth and the blanket the Prophet ﷺ had rested in. The most ordinary objects in her house had become the most precious, simply because of who had touched them.

She taught Hafsa (RA), the wife of the Prophet ﷺ, to read, to write, and to do calligraphy. There is a narration in which the Prophet ﷺ asked her to teach Hafsa a healing as well, the same way she had taught her to write. Later, the Quran was gathered and kept in the house of Hafsa, and the woman who taught her letters had a quiet hand in that.

After the Prophet ﷺ passed away, Umar (RA) still came to her home to seek her counsel on the affairs of the market, just as he had seen the Prophet ﷺ do. She lived into his time and then passed away, and it was Umar himself who led the funeral prayer over this early believer, healer, and teacher.
storybook.pullquote: A teacher rarely sees how far a single lesson will travel.
storybook.reflection: Al-Shifa was not remembered for one dramatic act. She was remembered for what she patiently gave: a skill passed on, a person taught. Knowledge shared with an open hand can outlive us by generations and reach places we will never see. If you understand something someone near you needs, give it.
storybook.source: Her story follows the account in Dr. Omar Suleiman's series The Firsts.
